beats.text: Every optimizer answers one question: given the gradient, how far and in what direction should the parameters step.
Plain stochastic gradient descent steps downhill by a fixed multiple of the gradient on a random mini-batch.

visual.note: one scalar learning rate for every parameter

beats.text: Momentum accumulates a decaying average of past gradients, so for a steady gradient at mu equals 0.9 the step grows tenfold.
Adam gives each parameter its own rate using a first and second moment, with bias correction at the start.
AdamW decouples weight decay, applying it directly to the weights outside the adaptive step, and generalizes better.
Adam holds two extra moments per parameter, adding eight bytes on top of the weight and gradient, the sixteen-bytes rule.
The learning rate is the most consequential hyperparameter, and the best value is not constant over a run.
The modern default ramps the rate up linearly during warmup, then eases it down along a half cosine.
Warmup gives the optimizer's adaptive statistics and a transformer's fragile early layers time to stabilize.

visual.note: linear warmup, commonly 1 to 5 percent of total steps

beats.text: Cosine decay is gentle at first, steepest in the middle, and flattens to the floor at the end of training.
For a ten thousand step run with three percent warmup, the linear phase lasts three hundred steps.
Warm restarts reset the schedule periodically, but with a good cosine they rarely help frontier single-run pretraining.
A network with millions of parameters can memorize its training set, so regularization pushes it to generalize instead.

visual.sub: fit the signal, not the noise

beats.text: Dropout zeros each activation with probability p during training and rescales the survivors so the expectation is unchanged.

visual.note: inference needs no rescaling, dropout is simply disabled

beats.text: A scale factor of 1.25 at train time corresponds to a keep probability of 0.8, dropping one activation in five.
The toolkit is small: weight decay, dropout, data augmentation, label smoothing, and early stopping.
Overfitting shows as a validation loss that bottoms out and rises while the training loss keeps falling.
Underfitting is the opposite: both losses sit high and flat because the model lacks capacity or training.
Modern GPUs run far faster in sixteen-bit and halve memory, so mixed precision keeps fp32 only where it is required.
The float16 format has only about five exponent bits, so its largest finite value is 65504 and small gradients underflow.
Loss scaling multiplies the loss by a large factor before backprop, then unscales the gradients before the step.

visual.note: S often 2^15; same math, range borrowed

beats.text: Three practices keep it safe: an fp32 master copy of weights, fp32 reductions, and bfloat16 where the hardware has it.
bfloat16 keeps fp32's eight exponent bits and a range near ten to the thirty-eighth, so it rarely needs loss scaling.
Most NaN failures are numeric, so gradient clipping that holds the norm at one is cheap standard insurance.
Theory converges, but in practice the failures are mundane and repetitive, so start from a default that survives reality.
The sane default is AdamW with weight decay 0.1, a learning rate near 3e-4, and a warmup-then-cosine schedule.

visual.note: bf16 if available, global-norm clip at 1.0

beats.text: Overfit one mini-batch first; if the loss cannot reach near zero, the bug is in the model, loss, or data pipeline.
Sanity-check the start: a ten-class classifier with random weights should begin near the natural log of ten.

visual.label: expected initial cross-entropy, ln K for K = 10

beats.text: Work the ladder cheapest first: overfit a batch, check initial loss, read the curve, sweep the learning rate, watch norms.
The learning rate dominates every other knob, so sweep it across orders of magnitude before touching architecture.
